stocks.note: Numbers as of market close March 29th
stocks.news:
Game engine maker Unity (U +13.54%) had a blowout session after forecasting stronger-than-expected Q1 sales, powered by its Vector AI ad platform that helps game developers find new users. It's a much-needed win for a stock that's been down nearly 60% this year.

Meanwhile, SpaceX is reportedly gearing up for a June IPO, with Elon Musk planning to offer 30% of shares to everyday investors — and rumor has it, shareholders might even get a front-row seat to rocket launches at Starbase, Texas. Because who needs dividends when you have fire and thrust?

economy.commentary:
Consumer sentiment just hit its lowest point of 2026, and the culprit is a familiar one: high oil prices and a stock market that can't seem to find its footing. The S&P 500 has shed roughly $5 trillion since the Iran conflict kicked off, and "extreme fear" is now the dominant mood on Wall Street.

In a plot twist nobody wanted, markets are now pricing in a greater chance of a Fed rate hike than a cut this year. Rising oil and import costs are stoking inflation fears, which means the Fed may have to tighten — even as the economy and job market slow down. It's the economic equivalent of having to run uphill in a headwind.

crypto.commentary:
Intercontinental Exchange — the company that literally owns the New York Stock Exchange — just dropped $600 million on Polymarket, the crypto-powered prediction platform where traders bet on everything from weather to elections. Regulators may not love it, but when the NYSE's parent company writes a check that big, it's a pretty strong vote of confidence.

In other news, U.S. crypto czar David Sacks has officially hung up his digital spurs, stepping down from his role overseeing federal crypto policy. He says he'll stick around to weigh in on AI topics, because apparently one emerging tech revolution wasn't enough.

real_estate.homebuilder: US homebuilder sentiment holds at 38 for March; better-than-expected but still under the 50 "break-even" mark as high interest rates and Middle East geopolitical tensions dampen buyer demand.
real_estate.commentary:
The Bay Area is seeing a "split personality" market this spring. While San Francisco and San Mateo are accelerating with homes moving in under 14 days, Santa Clara and Alameda counties are seeing inventory double since December, providing a rare window for buyers to negotiate without the typical frenzy.

📍 Latest Real Estate News in the Bay Area: As of today, Santa Clara County is a notable outlier in the regional market, showing a 3.5% year-over-year price dip even as demand picks up for the spring season. Active listings in the county have surged to 1,186 units — more than double the inventory seen in December — while closed sales are down 42% compared to last March, signaling that buyers are increasingly price-sensitive. In Alameda County (Fremont), the market remains the most accessible entry point in the region with a median price of $925,000, offering significantly more value than the Peninsula, where single-family homes in San Mateo are selling in as few as 8 days.

📍 Latest General News in the Bay Area: In Milpitas, hundreds gathered yesterday for an anti-Trump "No Kings" rally, protesting recent federal policy shifts as tensions over the Iran conflict enter their fifth week. The city also announced it will offer free doorbell cameras to residents starting this week to boost neighborhood safety. In Fremont, a critical Charter Advisory Committee meeting is scheduled for today at 5:00 PM to discuss the city's transition to a Charter City. Additionally, residents in both cities are navigating a holiday-shortened week with the state officially renaming Cesar Chavez Day to Farmworkers Day, a move that has prompted Milpitas to reconsider the future of Cesar Chavez Plaza.
